deconstruction_targets: secular judges' authority to try heresy and witchcraft cases
the distinction between heretics and apostates in relation to witchcraft
the legitimacy of civil punishment for religious offenses
constructive_proposals: establishing ecclesiastical jurisdiction over heresy and witchcraft trials
requiring secular judges to obtain ecclesiastical authorization before acting on such cases
recognizing that only the Bishop (ecclesiastical authority) can judge heresy, though secular execution may follow

scapegoat.name: wizards/heretics/apostates
attributes: apostates with intent to undermine Church doctrine
dangerous to the spiritual life of the soul
comparable or worse than counterfeiters and malefactors
scapegoat.justification: The text portrays wizards as apostates who intentionally corrupt the Faith—the life of the soul—making them more dangerous than mere criminals like counterfeiters, thus justifying their immediate execution.

moral_justification: Witchcraft is framed as a grave offense against the Faith itself, not merely civil crime; because heresy and apostasy threaten the spiritual survival of individuals and society, swift capital punishment is presented as morally necessary and equitable.
summary: The passage deconstructs secular claims to judicial authority in heresy and witchcraft cases by asserting ecclesiastical primacy while simultaneously advocating for severe punitive measures—especially death—for those accused of apostatic witchcraft, portraying them as existential threats whose elimination serves both religious and civil order.
